text.title: Contractor Evaluation and AI-Agent Gotchas
text.summary: Monthly evaluation rubric and agent-specific pitfalls when managing contractors with AI assistance.

section.title: Evaluation

rule.statement: Run a structured monthly review even when nothing is wrong. Rate the contractor on five areas (quality 1–5, communication 1–5, reliability 1–5, speed 1–5, proactiveness 1–5), document highlights and improvement areas, and make an explicit decision: continue, adjust scope/rate, or end engagement.
rule.rationale: Small frictions surface only when prompted. An informal "things seem fine" assessment misses gradual drift in quality, communication delays, or changes in availability that compound over quarters. An explicit decision prevents engagement inertia.

section.reference: Monthly evaluation template with rating rubric and decision framework

rule.statement: Maintain a "contractor onboarding kit" repo (Notion or git) that ships intact to every new contractor — versioned, reviewed quarterly.
rule.rationale: A versioned onboarding kit ensures consistency across contractors and reduces ramp-up time. It also forces documentation discipline that prevents institutional knowledge from existing only in the manager's head.

antipattern.description: LLM-generated feedback drifts toward mealy-mouthed praise without specific behavior + measurable impact.
antipattern.reason: Agents default to positive framing. Force SBI structure and require at least one concrete behavior + one measurable outcome in every feedback block.

antipattern.description: Agent over-trusts time-tracker self-reports without cross-checking against PR/commit/asset volume.
antipattern.reason: Time tracking is vulnerable to gaming. Before passing hours to evaluation, cross-check reported time against verifiable output volume.

antipattern.description: Agent digests Slack history and flattens sarcasm and friction signals.
antipattern.reason: Any thread with more than 5 back-and-forth messages indicates friction that requires human reading, not summarization.

antipattern.description: Agent auto-paying invoices or auto-approving payment without human review.
antipattern.reason: Cross-border payments on disputed work are hard to claw back. Each invoice requires explicit human approval against the SOW.

antipattern.description: Agent pulling Slack or Notion into LLM context without redaction of customer data.
antipattern.reason: Status threads frequently reference customer names, deal sizes, or support issues. Use a redaction step before any external-API LLM call.

antipattern.description: Auto-generated SOW addenda re-introducing default IP/confidentiality clauses that conflict with the master agreement.
antipattern.reason: Always diff any agent-generated addendum against the signed master agreement before sending.
